content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 18, 1998

Richard Radan
7418 Midron Drive
Fair Oaks, California 95628

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-98-041

Dear Mr. Radan:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because you have not provided specific facts concerning the
duties of your future position and the projects you manage in your present position, we are
treating your request as one for informal assistance.² (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(C), copy
enclosed.)

QUESTIONS

1. May you contact your former state employer for business purposes after leaving state
service?

2. May you leave state service and continue to work on contracts with outside vendors
that you managed for the agency?

3. May you accept a position offered by a private company?

4. May you communicate with former co-workers socially?

CONCLUSIONS

1. For one year after leaving state service, you may not communicate, for compensation,
with your former agency in an attempt to influence any administrative or legislative action, or to

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity conferred by formal written advice.
(Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-98-041
Page 2

influence the issuance, amendment, awarding or revocation of a permit, license, contract, or
transaction involving property or goods.

2. You may not appear, for compensation, before your former agency in a proceeding
regarding a contract or any other judicial or quasi-judicial proceeding in which you participated
as a former state employee. You are also prohibited from being paid to aid, advise, counsel,
consult or assist in representing any other person in such proceedings.

3. The Act does not preclude you from accepting a position in the private sector upon
leaving state service. However, prior to leaving state service you may not make, participate in
making or use your official position to influence a decision directly related to your prospective
employer.

4. You may communicate with former co-workers socially as long as you do not
communicate with them in a manner that would violate sections 87401 and 87406, which apply
to formal and informal appearances before an agency.

FACTS

You are considering leaving state service and accepting employment with a private
company. You work at Health & Welfare Data Center ("HWDC") as a deputy director (CEA II)
managing several large information technology projects. During the last five years, you have
managed projects resulting from contracts between HWDC and a prospective employer. You
have been offered a position in the private sector. This position is not directly related to the work
you do for the state. However, you will be working with the public sector.

ANALYSIS

The Act does not preclude you from accepting a position in the private sector upon
leaving state service. However, the Act does place three restrictions on the activities of state
officials who are leaving state service. Two are post-employment, while the third regulates
activities of the official before leaving.

One-Year "Revolving Door" Ban

Section 87406(d)(1) of the Act provides in pertinent part:

"No designated employee of a state administrative agency ... for a
period of one year after leaving office or employment, shall, for
compensation, act as agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent,
any other person, by making any formal or informal appearance, or
by making any oral or written communication, before any state
administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof, for which he
or she worked or represented during the 12 months before leaving

File No. 1-98-041
Page 3

office or employment, if the appearance or communication is made
for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action,
or influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance,
amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or
contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property."

Accordingly, as a designated employee,³ you are prohibited, for one year after leaving
state service, from being paid to communicate with HWDC in an attempt to influence any
administrative or legislative action, or to influence the issuance, amendment, awarding or
revocation of a permit, license, contract, or transaction involving property or goods. This
prohibition applies to formal and informal appearances, and to oral or written communications.

However, general communications to state administrative agencies, which are not for the
purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action, or influencing any action or
proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,
grant, or contract, or the transaction or purchase of goods or property, are not restricted. Thus,
you may contact individuals from HWDC socially as long as you do not attempt to influence
them on any action or proceeding as set forth in section 87406.

Permanent Ban on "Switching Sides"

Sections 87401 and 87402 provide additional restrictions on the post-governmental
employment activity of former public officials that may apply where the one-year prohibition in
section 87406 does not, or where the one-year prohibition has ended.

Section 87401 provides:

"No state administrative official,⁴ after termination of his or her
employment or term of office, shall for compensation act as agent
or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person (other than
the State of California) before any court or state administrative
agency or any officer or employee thereof by making any formal or

___________________________

³ A "designated employee" includes any officer, employee, member, or consultant of any agency whose
position with the agency is: 1) exempt from the state civil service system unless the position is elective or solely
secretarial, clerical or manual; 2) elective, other than an elective state office; 3) designated in a Conflict of Interest
Code; or 4) involved at other than a clerical or ministerial level in the functions of negotiating or signing any
contract awarded through competitive bidding or executed pursuant to section 10122 of the Public Contract Code.
(Section 82019.)

⁴ A "state administrative official" means every member officer, employee of consultant of a state
administrative agency who engages in any judicial or quasi-judicial proceedings in other than a purely clerical,
secretarial, or ministerial capacity. (Section 87400(b).)

File No. I-98-041
Page 4

informal appearance, or by making any oral or written communication with the intent to influence, in connection with any judicial
or quasi-judicial or other proceeding if both of the following apply:
(a) The State of California is a party or has a direct and
substantial interest.
(b) The proceeding is one in which the former state
administrative official participated."

In addition, section 87402 prohibits former state administrative officials from being paid
to aid, advise, counsel, consult or assist in representing any other person in any proceeding in
which the official would be prohibited from appearing under section 87401.

The permanent ban of sections 87401 and 87402 applies only to judicial, quasi-judicial,
or other proceedings before any court or administrative agency in which a former employee
participated while at his or her former agency.

A "judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding" means:

"[A]ny proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other
determination, contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge,
accusation, arrest or other particular matter involving a specific
party or parties in any court or state administrative agency ...."
(Section 87400(c), emphasis added.)

An official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if the official took part in
the proceeding "personally and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal
written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation or use of
confidential information." (Section 87400(d).) The permanent ban applies to any proceeding in
which the official has actually participated while at his or her former agency, as well as any
proceeding which the official supervised. (Brown Advice Letter, No. A-91-033.)

When an official is involved in the review, selection, funding, monitoring or performance
of a contract between his or her agency and a specific party, the official is "participating" in a
proceeding as defined in section 87400. (Chacon Advice Letter, No. A-87-197.) However, the
permanent ban does not restrict a former state employee's ability to participate in any new
proceedings involving the same parties. In addition, we have advised that the contract
application and award process is a separate proceeding from the contract monitoring process.
(Blonien Advice Letter, No. A-89-463.)

Accordingly, you are prohibited from being paid to appear before HWDC in a proceeding
regarding a contract or any other judicial or quasi-judicial proceeding in which you participated
as a former state employee. You are also prohibited from being paid to aid, advise, counsel,
consult or assist in representing any other person in such proceedings.

File No. 1-98-041
Page 5

Influencing Prospective Employment

In addition to the revolving door provisions of the Act, section 87407 prohibits a state
administrative official from making, participating in making, or using his or her official position
to influence, any governmental decision directly relating to any person whom the official is
negotiating, or has any arrangement concerning, prospective employment.

For additional guidance, I have enclosed a fact sheet regarding the revolving door and
other related restrictions in the Act. If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please
contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Julia Butcher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:JB:tls

Enclosures
